The account the psalmist gives here of his troubles applies very well to Christ: many hated him without cause; indeed, the Lord himself chastened him severely, bruised him, and caused him grief, so that by his wounds we might be healed. God is sometimes the strength of his people when he is not their joy; they have spiritual supports even though they lack spiritual delights. Whether a believer traces back his comfort to God’s everlasting goodness and mercy, or whether he looks forward to the blessing secured to him, he will find abundant reason for joy and praise. Every answer to our prayers shows that the Lord is on our side; then we need not fear what people can do to us; we should conscientiously do our duty to all, and trust in him alone to accept and bless us. Let us seek to live to declare the works of God, and to encourage others to serve him and trust in him. Such were the triumphs of David’s son, in the assurance that the good pleasure of the Lord would prosper in his hand.

Those who saw Christ's day at so great a distance, saw reason to praise God for the prospect. The prophecy, verse #( ), may refer to David’s promotion; but principally to Christ. 1. His humiliation: he is the Stone which the builders refused: they would continue in their building without him. This proved the ruin of those who thus made light of him. Rejecters of Christ are rejected by God. 2. His exaltation: he is the chief Cornerstone in the foundation. He is the chief Top-stone, in whom the building is completed, who must, in all things, have preeminence. Christ’s name is Wonderful; and the redemption he accomplished is the most amazing of all God's wondrous works. We will rejoice and be glad in the Lord’s day, not only that such a day is appointed, but because of the occasion of it, Christ becoming the Head. Sabbath days ought to be rejoicing days, then they are to us as the days of heaven. Let this Savior be my Savior, my Ruler. Let my soul prosper and be in health, in that peace and righteousness which his government brings. Let me have victory over the lusts that war against my soul; and let Divine grace subdue my heart. The duty the Lord has made brings light with it, true light. The duty this privilege calls for is set forth here; the sacrifices we are to offer to God in gratitude for redeeming love are ourselves: not to be slain upon the altar, but living sacrifices, to be bound to the altar; spiritual sacrifices of prayer and praise, in which our hearts must be engaged. The psalmist praises God, and calls on all around him to give thanks to God for the glad tidings of great joy to all people, that there is a Redeemer, even Christ the Lord. In him the covenant of grace is made sure and everlasting.
